title: Use the Pomodoro Technique
body: Study for 25 minutes, then take a 5-minute break. After four cycles, take a longer 15–30 minute break. This method prevents burnout and keeps your focus sharp during long study sessions.

title: Review Lecture Notes Within 24 Hours
body: Research shows that revisiting notes within 24 hours of a lecture dramatically improves long-term retention. Even a 10-minute review before bed can make a significant difference during exams.

title: Create a Dedicated Study Space
body: Your brain associates environments with activities. Having a consistent, clutter-free study space signals to your mind that it's time to focus. Avoid studying in bed — it blurs the line between rest and work.

title: Practice Active Recall
body: Instead of passively re-reading notes, close the book and write down everything you remember. This retrieval practice is one of the most effective study techniques proven by cognitive science.

title: Plan Your Week Every Sunday
body: Spend 15 minutes each Sunday mapping out your deadlines, lectures, and personal goals for the week ahead. A clear plan reduces anxiety and helps you stay ahead of last-minute stress.

title: Keep a Consistent Sleep Schedule
body: Going to bed and waking up at the same time each day — even on weekends — helps regulate your internal clock. Consistency is more important than duration for the quality of your sleep.

title: Avoid Screens 1 Hour Before Bed
body: The blue light emitted by phones and laptops suppresses melatonin production, making it harder to fall asleep. Try switching to a book, light stretching, or calming music in your final hour before bed.

title: Cool Your Room Down
body: Your body temperature naturally drops as you fall asleep. A cool room between 16–19°C (60–67°F) signals to your body that it's time to sleep and significantly improves sleep depth.

title: Try a 'Brain Dump' Before Bed
body: If racing thoughts keep you awake, spend five minutes writing down everything on your mind in a notebook. This offloads mental clutter and gives your brain permission to rest.

title: Limit Caffeine After 2 PM
body: Caffeine has a half-life of about 5–6 hours, meaning a 3 PM coffee still has half its potency at 9 PM. Cutting off caffeine in the early afternoon is one of the fastest wins for better sleep.

title: Schedule Social Time Like a Class
body: When life gets busy, social connection is often the first thing dropped. Block time in your calendar for friends the same way you would for a lecture — your mental health depends on it.

title: Join One Club or Society
body: Research consistently shows that belonging to at least one campus community dramatically reduces feelings of loneliness and isolation. It doesn't have to be a big commitment — even attending once a month makes a difference.

title: Practice Being Fully Present
body: When spending time with friends, put your phone away. Studies show that just having a phone visible on the table reduces the quality of conversation and feelings of connection.

title: Reach Out First
body: Waiting for others to reach out can lead to prolonged isolation. A simple 'Hey, how are you doing?' text takes seconds and can be the start of a meaningful reconnection. Most people appreciate it more than you'd expect.

title: Be Kind to Yourself in Social Situations
body: Social anxiety is extremely common among university students. If you feel awkward or out of place, remember that most people are focused on themselves, not judging you. Embrace imperfection — it makes you more relatable.
